title: a mounting bracket with four holes

turns.text: The kernel centers primitives on the origin and takes half-extents, so a 60×40×4 base is `box3(30, 20, 2)` and the back wall is `box3(30, 2, 20)` stood up along −Y. I’ll union them, then punch four Ø6 (r=3) holes through the base on a rectangular pattern — the pattern is just a JS loop.

args.name: angle-bracket

turns.text: `create_model` returns immediately with `valid: true` and volume 18032 mm³ — the four holes and the boolean union all resolved into one closed solid. Let me eyeball it before exporting.
That matches the spec. Mass properties, then the files:
Volume 18032 mm³ = 18.0 cm³; in 6061 aluminium (2.70 g/cm³) that’s about **49 g**. Exporting STEP and STL:
Done — a valid, watertight bracket with four through-holes, delivered as an analytic-fallback STEP file and a print-ready STL, straight from the prompt.

title: a hinge leaf with knuckles and a pin bore

turns.text: A knuckle is a cylinder whose default +Z axis I rotate onto +X, then slide along X. Three of them union onto the plate; a long thin cylinder on the same axis subtracts the pin bore. `rotate(0, 1, 0, 90)` swings +Z onto +X.

args.name: hinge-leaf

turns.text: Valid solid, 21,368 triangles — the pin bore runs cleanly through all three knuckles. Let me look at it and confirm the mesh is watertight before exporting.
`closedManifold: true`, no issues — a real solid, not a surface soup. Exporting STEP for the mechanical model:
Mirror this leaf about X and pin the two together and you have a working hinge.

title: a shelled enclosure with a press-fit lid

turns.text: I’ll shell the body by subtracting an inner cavity from a rounded box, raising the cavity so it breaks through the top face — that leaves the box open for the lid. Then a separate lid with a recessed lip sized for a press fit.

args.name: enclosure-body

turns.text: Body is valid — volume 34621 mm³ after hollowing. Rendering it to check the wall broke through the top:
Open-topped shell, confirmed. Now the lid: a top plate with a lip that drops into the mouth with 0.5 mm clearance per wall.

args.name: enclosure-lid

turns.text: Two parts, both watertight: an open-topped 3 mm-walled enclosure and a press-fit lid, each as STEP and STL. The lip is 0.5 mm under the cavity on every wall, so it seats without jamming.

title: a toothed disk from a circular pattern

turns.text: One tooth box, rotated into 16 positions by a loop, unioned onto a root cylinder, minus a central bore. `(360 * i) / TEETH` spaces the teeth evenly.

args.name: gear-disk

turns.text: All 16 teeth resolved — `valid: true`, volume 9000 mm³. Top view to check the tooth count and spacing, then iso:
Sixteen evenly-spaced teeth. STL exports the mesh directly:

For completeness I’ll also try STEP. The thin teeth sit right at the edge of the model’s bounding box, and the faceted STEP path needs the surface strictly *inside* the meshing region — so this is a case where the export declines rather than emitting a broken file. Watch how it reports:

turns.error: Error: export failed: STEP export failed: degenerate geometry in sdf_to_brep: adaptive meshing did not produce a closed manifold; the surface must lie strictly inside the meshing bounds

turns.text: That’s the honest failure mode: `isError: true` with a specific reason, not a silently-corrupt STEP. The STL is unaffected — meshing and STEP’s planar-region recovery are different code paths. For an analytic STEP of a gear you’d thicken the teeth slightly or model them as an extruded `Profile`; the STL here is already print-ready.

title: a bottle from a revolved, shelled profile

turns.text: A `Profile` is a closed polyline with optional arcs. I draw the outer silhouette — straight up the body, an `arcTo` for the rounded shoulder, up the neck — revolve it about Y, then subtract a slightly-smaller inner revolve that runs taller than the neck so it opens the mouth. That inner subtract is the shell.

args.name: bottle

turns.text: Rendering the result from the front and iso to check the silhouette and the open neck:
Now the watertightness check — a shell that didn’t break through would read as a closed cavity:
`closedManifold: true`, volume 21531 mm³ — a genuine hollow solid with an open mouth. Exporting mesh formats:
A hollow bottle with a rounded shoulder and an open neck, watertight where it should be and open where it should be — all from a revolved profile and one boolean subtract.

title: a right-angle bracket with a gusset and filleted corner

turns.text: Two kernel facts drive the whole script. First, the extrusion axis is **+Y**, not +Z: `Shape.extrude` sweeps a profile’s `(u, v)` into `(x, z)` and pushes it along +Y (`bounded.rs`), and `Shape.cylinder` is likewise a **+Y**-axis cylinder — radial in xz, axial in y (`primitives.rs`). Second, that makes the L-section a *profile* problem: I can draw the whole cross-section in (x, z) — including the 3 mm interior fillet as a real arc — and sweep it 40 mm along +Y for the width. `arcTo`’s bulge is the DXF convention, `tan(sweep/4)`, so a 90° arc is `tan(22.5°) = 0.4142`; negative sweeps it clockwise, which is the concave direction here.

args.name: bracket-right-angle

turns.text: `valid: true`, volume 19726 mm³. That is the oracle that matters: hand-integrating the section gives 19792 mm³ (19077 for the filleted L, +1000 gusset, +blend, −393 for four Ø5 holes through 5 mm), so the mesh is reading 0.3% under — the same bias a plain 60×40×5 slab shows (11968 vs 12000). The holes are real: drop them and the body measures 20184 mm³. Let me look at it from three sides before exporting.
The front view shows the L with the filleted interior corner, the right view shows the gusset blended into both plates, and the iso shows all four holes. Mass properties:

Volume 19726 mm³ = 19.7 cm³; in 6061 aluminium (2.70 g/cm³) that is about **53 g**. Note the `boundingBox` the tools report is the *tracked* box, not the part: it reads 61.5 × 41.5 × 41.5 for a part that is really 60 × 40 × 40, because `smoothUnion` pads the tracked box by `radius/4` and the no-op rotation pads it further. Do not trust it as a measurement. Exporting:

A valid, watertight right-angle bracket — gusset blended, corner filleted, four M5 holes drilled on two axes — as a faceted STEP and a print-ready STL. Two caveats an agent should carry forward: the **+Y** axis convention for `extrude`/`cylinder` (get this wrong and holes silently become channels, with no error), and the fact that faceted STEP export succeeded here only because of the trailing no-op rotation — the identical part without it meshes open and declines to export.
